steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The team concludes that exposure drives the ability to distinguish familiar from unfamiliar languages. A supporting finding must show that greater exposure produces stronger distinction ability.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'older dogs show the greatest brain-activity difference between familiar and unfamiliar languages': Older dogs have accumulated more exposure over time, and their larger brain-activity gap directly links exposure to sharper distinction ability. ✓
  • Choice B — 'dogs react to scrambled non-speech': Scrambled non-speech is not an unfamiliar language, so this does not test the familiar-vs-unfamiliar distinction the conclusion describes. ✗
  • Choice C — 'the distinction pattern is uniform across ages': Uniform results across ages would suggest exposure has no effect, undercutting rather than supporting the team's claim. ✗
  • Choice D — 'dogs react similarly to scrambled and unfamiliar speech': Again involves scrambled non-speech rather than the language-vs-language comparison needed. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
